content:
You are a knowledge graph extraction agent. Read the conversation transcript below and extract entities and relationships.

## Entity Types
person, project, technology, concept, file, decision, task, problem

## Relationship Types
uses, depends_on, built_with, related_to, part_of, created, modified, solved, caused, blocked_by, decided, worked_on, learned, prefers

## Output
Read existing entities from /home/mohan/.claude/knowledge-graph/entities.jsonl first to avoid duplicates (match by name+type).
Append new entities to /home/mohan/.claude/knowledge-graph/entities.jsonl — one JSON per line:
{"name":"X","type":"technology","description":"...","properties":{},"session_id":"web-26ad29a9-25f7-4e22-8bf4-8a625b7b82ef","date":"2026-03-20"}

Append new relationships to /home/mohan/.claude/knowledge-graph/relationships.jsonl — one JSON per line:
{"source":"X","target":"Y","type":"uses","context":"...","session_id":"web-26ad29a9-25f7-4e22-8bf4-8a625b7b82ef","date":"2026-03-20"}

## Rules
- Skip trivial/generic entities. Focus on meaningful things.
- Keep descriptions under 100 chars.
- Do NOT modify existing lines — only append.
- If the conversation has no meaningful entities, write nothing.

## Conversation Transcript
Read the file at: /home/mohan/.claude/knowledge-graph/tmpxf2eaij7.txt

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- session-logger:renameAI: AI-assisted session rename - generates a concise, descriptive name from conversation context.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

User: Create an invitation message for our daughter's sweet 16 party. This message will be sent to my daughter's friends as well as my friends & family.
Assistant:  Mohan is planning a Sweet 16 for Amukta Duggi. Let me create a nice invitation message. I'll ask a few key questions first to personalize it well.I'd love to help craft the perfect invitation! A few quick questions to make it just right:
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

Waiting for user response.Go ahead and share those details — and feel free to drop the date, time, and venue directly in your reply too!
User: Q: What's the party date, time, and location?
A: Just use placeholders for now

Q: What vibe should the invitation have?
A: Elegant & formal

Q: Any extras to mention? (Select all that apply)
A: [No preference]
Assistant:  The user wants an elegant & formal Sweet 16 invitation for Amukta Duggi, with placeholders for date/time/location, and no extras needed.Here are two versions — one for close friends & family, and one tailored for your daughter's friends:
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

I gave you two versions to match the different audiences:

- **For friends & family** — warm and formal, fitting for adults who'll appreciate the sentimental tone
- **For Amukta's friends** — a touch more upbeat and personal while still being polished

Just fill in the bracketed placeholders and you're good to go! Let me know if you'd like to adjust the tone, wording, or add any details like a theme or dress code.

python3 << 'PYTHON_EOF'
import json

# Entities to add
entities = [
    {"name":"Mohan","type":"person","description":"User planning a Sweet 16 party for daughter Amukta","properties":{},"session_id":"6bf23bab-de14-461a-9547-039413130227","date":"2026-04-17"},
    {"name":"Amukta Duggi","type":"person","description":"Mohan's daughter, celebrating Sweet 16 party","properties":{},"session_id":"6bf23bab-de14-461a-9547-039413130227","date":"2026-04-17"},
    {"name":"Sweet 16 Party","type":"task","description":"Birthday celebration party for Amukta Duggi with guests from family and friends","properties":{"tone":"Elegant & formal"},"session_id":"6bf23bab-de14-461a-9547-039413130227","date":"2026-04-17"},
    {"name":"Invitation Message Creation","type":"task","description":"Create personalized invitation for Sweet 16 party with two versions for different audiences","properties":{"audiences":["close friends & family","daughter's friends"],"tone":"Elegant & formal"},"session_id":"6bf23bab-de14-461a-9547-039413130227","date":"2026-04-17"}
]

# Relationships to add
relationships = [
    {"source":"Mohan","target":"Sweet 16 Party","type":"worked_on","context":"Planning daughter Amukta's Sweet 16 party celebration","session_id":"6bf23bab-de14-461a-9547-039413130227","date":"2026-04-17"},
    {"source":"Mohan","target":"Invitation Message Creation","type":"worked_on","context":"Creating invitation messages for the Sweet 16 party","session_id":"6bf23bab-de14-461a-9547-039413130227","date":"2026-04-17"},
    {"source":"Amukta Duggi","target":"Sweet 16 Party","type":"part_of","context":"Birthday celebrant for the Sweet 16 party","session_id":"6bf23bab-de14-461a-9547-039413130227","date":"2026-04-17"},
    {"source":"Invitation Message Creation","target":"Sweet 16 Party","type":"part_of","context":"Invitations are component of the party planning","session_id":"6bf23bab-de14-461a-9547-039413130227","date":"2026-04-17"},
    {"source":"Mohan","target":"Elegant & formal tone","type":"decided","context":"Chose elegant and formal tone for Sweet 16 invitation messages","session_id":"6bf23bab-de14-461a-9547-039413130227","date":"2026-04-17"}
]

# Append entities
with open('/home/mohan/.claude/knowledge-graph/entities.jsonl', 'a') as f:
    for entity in entities:
        f.write(json.dumps(entity) + '\n')

# Append relationships
with open('/home/mohan/.claude/knowledge-graph/relationships.jsonl', 'a') as f:
    for rel in relationships:
        f.write(json.dumps(rel) + '\n')

print("Extraction complete: 4 entities and 5 relationships added")
PYTHON_EOF
